questions.question: What is the signature of a reducer function used with `useReducer`?
options: `(state, action) => nextState`
`(action, state) => void`
`(prevState, nextState) => action`
`(state) => (action) => nextState`
questions.explanation: A reducer is a pure function `(state, action) => nextState`. It receives the current state and an action and returns the new state; it does not mutate or take state second.

questions.question: Which of the following are true of a well-written reducer? (Select all that apply.)
options: It must be pure: same inputs always yield the same output
It should perform no side effects or I/O
It should return a new object/array rather than mutating the existing state
It should issue API calls when an action requires fetching data
It is allowed to read the current time or random values to compute next state
questions.explanation: Reducers are pure: no mutation, no side effects, no I/O, and deterministic. Making API calls or reading the clock/random values inside a reducer breaks purity and reproducibility.

questions.question: React guarantees that the `dispatch` function returned by `useReducer` keeps a stable identity across renders, so it is safe to omit from effect dependency arrays.
questions.explanation: `dispatch` identity is guaranteed constant across renders, so it can be omitted from effect deps and passed through context without causing extra re-renders.

questions.question: Given `useReducer(reducer, initialArg, init)`, how does React use the third argument `init`?
options: React calls `init(initialArg)` once on mount to compute the initial state
React calls `init` after every dispatch to recompute state from scratch
React passes `init` to the reducer as a fourth parameter
React uses `init` only when no provider supplies a value
questions.explanation: The optional `init` function enables lazy initialization: React calls `init(initialArg)` once on mount to compute the initial state, avoiding recomputing it on every render. It is not invoked on each dispatch.

questions.question: Which situation most favors `useReducer` over `useState`?
options: Many interrelated state updates spread across several event handlers that you want centralized and unit-testable
A single boolean toggle local to one component
Two independent numeric values updated with simple setters
State that never changes after the initial render
questions.explanation: `useReducer` wins when updates are many, interrelated, or span event handlers, and when you want the update logic out of the component and testable. One or two independent simple values favor `useState`.

questions.question: Which dispatch call best follows the principle that an action should describe intent, not mechanism?
options: `dispatch({ type: 'added_todo', text })`
`dispatch(nextWholeStateObject)`
`dispatch(() => setState(newState))`
`dispatch(state => ({ ...state, todos: newTodos }))`
questions.explanation: Actions should describe *what happened* (intent), e.g. `{ type: 'added_todo', text }`, letting the reducer own *how* state changes. Passing the whole next state or an updater function moves the 'how' back out of the centralized reducer.

questions.question: When is the default value passed to `createContext(default)` actually used?
options: Only when a consumer has no matching provider above it in the tree
Every time the provider's value is `undefined`
On the first render, before any provider mounts
Whenever `React.memo` blocks a re-render of an intermediate component
questions.explanation: The default is used only when a consumer renders with no matching provider above it. If a provider exists, the consumer reads the provider's value even if that value is `undefined`.

questions.question: Wrapping an intermediate component in `React.memo` prevents the context consumers below it from re-rendering when the context provider's value changes.
questions.explanation: When a provider's value changes (by `Object.is`), every consumer re-renders regardless of depth, and `React.memo` on intermediate components does not stop this. memo only short-circuits re-renders caused by changed props, not context changes.

questions.question: A provider is written as `<ThemeContext value={{ user, theme }}>`. Why might this re-render all consumers on every render of the provider's component?
options: A fresh inline object is created each render, so the value fails the `Object.is` comparison every time
Context always re-renders consumers on every render regardless of the value
Inline objects bypass React's reconciliation and force a full tree remount
`useContext` cannot read object values, only primitives
questions.explanation: Passing a fresh inline object/array each render produces a new reference that fails `Object.is`, so React treats the value as changed and re-renders all consumers. Memoizing the value (e.g. `useMemo`) fixes it.

questions.question: Which use case is context best suited for, per the reviewer?
options: Global-ish low-frequency data like theme, current user/auth, or locale
High-frequency state such as text being typed into an input
Replacing all local prop passing throughout the app
Server cache for paginated API responses
questions.explanation: Context is for global-ish data (theme, auth, locale, design tokens). It is not a general state manager, not a replacement for local props, and overusing it couples components and hurts reuse.

questions.question: Why does the reducer+context scaling pattern split into a separate `StateContext` and `DispatchContext` instead of one `{ state, dispatch }` context?
options: So dispatch-only components subscribe to the stable `dispatch` and never re-render on state changes
Because a single context cannot hold both a value and a function
Because `dispatch` must be provided above `state` to be readable
To allow two providers to override each other's defaults
questions.explanation: Combining `{ state, dispatch }` creates a new value identity each render and re-renders every consumer on any state change, including dispatch-only ones. Splitting lets dispatch-only components read the stable `dispatch` and stay inert.

questions.question: In the `TasksProvider` pattern, the state context is initialized with `createContext<Task[] | null>(null)` and `useTasks()` throws if the value is `null`. What bug does the throw catch?
options: Using the hook outside the provider, which would otherwise silently return the `null` default
Dispatching an unknown action type from a child component
Passing a non-memoized object as the provider value
Calling `useReducer` more than once in the same provider
questions.explanation: If a component reads the context outside any `TasksProvider`, `useContext` returns the `null` default. The throw turns that silent failure into a loud, immediate error ('must be used within TasksProvider') instead of handing back null.

questions.question: Given the reducer below, what does `reducer(5, { type: 'set', value: 10 })` return?
options: It throws an 'unknown action' error
questions.explanation: The `set` case returns `action.value`, which is `10`, ignoring the previous state of `5`. The `incremented` case (which would give `6`) does not run, and `set` is a known type so it does not throw.

questions.question: What is the key re-render advantage a store library (Redux Toolkit / Zustand) has over a single reducer+context value?
options: A component subscribes to a slice via a selector and re-renders only when that slice changes, not on every state change
Stores never re-render components; they update the DOM directly
Stores avoid React's reconciliation entirely by using the virtual DOM differently
Stores guarantee components re-render synchronously before paint
questions.explanation: A context provider re-renders all consumers when its value changes. A store lets each component subscribe to a slice via a selector and re-render only when that slice changes. Both also subscribe via `useSyncExternalStore` for consistency under concurrent rendering.

questions.question: Which statements about Redux Toolkit (RTK) are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: `createSlice` generates both a reducer and its action creators from one object
RTK uses Immer internally so 'mutating' code like `state.value++` becomes a safe immutable update
`configureStore` enables thunk middleware and devtools by default
RTK requires you to hand-write `createStore` and action type constants
Reducers written with `createSlice` must manually return a new state object and never mutate the draft
questions.explanation: `createSlice` generates a reducer plus action creators and uses Immer, so 'mutating' the draft (`state.value++`) is turned into an immutable update; `configureStore` wires thunks + devtools by default. With RTK you essentially never write plain `createStore` or hand-rolled action types, and mutating the Immer draft is exactly the intended style.

questions.question: Which statements about Zustand (v5) are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: `create` returns a hook that is itself the store; no `<Provider>` is needed
`set` shallow-merges the top level of the store state
It can be used outside React as a vanilla store
It requires wrapping the app in a `<Provider store>` like React-Redux
Its default export is the primary way to create a store in v5
questions.explanation: Zustand's `create` returns a store-hook with no provider, `set` shallow-merges the top level, and v5 works outside React as a vanilla store. It needs no `<Provider>`, and v5 removed the default export in favor of the named `{ create }`.

questions.question: A Zustand selector returns a fresh object each call: `useStore(s => ({ a: s.a, b: s.b }))`. What problem can this cause and how is it fixed?
options: It can trigger re-render loops because a new object fails equality each time; wrap it with `useShallow` (or use `createWithEqualityFn`)
It silently returns stale values; fix it by calling the hook twice
It throws at runtime because selectors may only return primitives
It disables the store's subscription; fix it by adding a `<Provider>`
questions.explanation: Returning a new object/array from a selector each call produces a new reference that fails the default equality check, causing re-render loops. `useShallow` (or `createWithEqualityFn` for custom equality) compares the contents to prevent it.

questions.question: For state that is purely local to one component, which option is recommended?
options: None of context, Zustand, or Redux Toolkit â€” keep it local (e.g. `useState`/`useReducer`)
Context, to make it available if a child ever needs it
Redux Toolkit, for devtools and structure
Zustand, because it has the least boilerplate
questions.explanation: The reviewer says to reach for none of context/Zustand/RTK for state that is purely local to one component. Context suits global-ish values, Zustand a small fast store, and RTK large structured apps â€” but local state stays local.
